texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years ionic liquid membranes have gained relevance and were proven to effectively separate compounds of interest. The first approach was developed by Branco et al. [1], who proposed to use ionic liquids (ILs) as bulk membranes, in a U-shaped tube, or as supported ionic liquid membranes (SILMs), by impregnating a porous membrane with the desired ionic liquid. With this approach it was possible to separate secondary and tertiary amines with very similar boiling points, which was not possible to do before either with other liquid membranes or by distillation. Since then, many substances have been separated with SILMs, such as hydrocarbons, alcohols, esters and gases [2-5].
texts.text: In recent years ionic liquid membranes have gained relevance and were proven to effectively separate compounds of interest. The first approach was developed by Branco et al. [1], who proposed to use ionic liquids (ILs) as bulk membranes, in a U-shaped tube, or as supported ionic liquid membranes (SILMs), by impregnating a porous membrane with the desired ionic liquid. With this approach it was possible to separate secondary and tertiary amines with very similar boiling points, which was not possible to do before either with other liquid membranes or by distillation. Since then, many substances have been separated with SILMs, such as hydrocarbons, alcohols, esters and gases [2-5].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are mainly three approaches commonly used to immobilise IL in a porous support: (1) direct immersion, in which the membrane is soaked in the IL [5-8]; (2) pressure, in which the IL is spread over the membrane and a small pressure is applied to force it to flow into the membrane pores [9,10]; (3) and vacuum, in which the air in the pores of the supporting membrane is removed with vacuum and then, still under vacuum, IL is spread over the membrane [1,10-14]. In all the cases excess IL has to be removed from the surface of the membrane, either by letting it drip, or by gently wiping it with absorbing tissue. In a study where the operational stability was assessed, it was found that the preparation method in which pressure is used, results in higher amounts of IL immobilized in the membrane and that also presents higher stability [10].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work for the first time Ion-Jelly Ò membranes were developed and characterized. Ion-Jelly Ò is a new green and sustainable gel material, obtained by addition of an ionic liquid to gelatine. Three different ionic liquids ([EMIM][DCA], [BMIM][DCA] and [BMPyr][DCA]), were used to study the difference in the membrane properties introduced by changes in the ionic liquid cation. The physical properties of the membranes were characterized by traction, contact angles, and SEM. These membranes were further tested in separation processes using supercritical carbon dioxide for the fractionation of a model mixture made of oleic acid and squalene and for the separation of biodiesel products and reagents. The permeability to pure gases (H2, N2, O2, CO2 and CH4) was also determined and ideal selectivities calculated.
texts.text: In this work for the first time Ion-Jelly Ò membranes were developed and characterized. Ion-Jelly Ò is a new green and sustainable gel material, obtained by addition of an ionic liquid to gelatine. Three different ionic liquids ([EMIM][DCA], [BMIM][DCA] and [BMPyr][DCA]), were used to study the difference in the membrane properties introduced by changes in the ionic liquid cation. The physical properties of the membranes were characterized by traction, contact angles, and SEM. These membranes were further tested in separation processes using supercritical carbon dioxide for the fractionation of a model mixture made of oleic acid and squalene and for the separation of biodiesel products and reagents. The permeability to pure gases (H2, N2, O2, CO2 and CH4) was also determined and ideal selectivities calculated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is known that ILs are displaced from SILMs under operation, even at low differential pressure (above 2 bar) [14]. To try to overcome this difficulty, gel membranes, in which IL is blended with a polymer, have been prepared by several authors [15-17]. Membranes prepared in this way retain solubility characteristics identical to the liquid, which confer them identical permeabilities, but with improved mechanical properties. These membranes were effectively employed in the separation of gases such as carbon dioxide from nitrogen [15,17], methane [16,17], and hydrogen [17] or hydrogen from nitrogen [17].
texts.text: It is known that ILs are displaced from SILMs under operation, even at low differential pressure (above 2 bar) [14]. To try to overcome this difficulty, gel membranes, in which IL is blended with a polymer, have been prepared by several authors [15-17]. Membranes prepared in this way retain solubility characteristics identical to the liquid, which confer them identical permeabilities, but with improved mechanical properties. These membranes were effectively employed in the separation of gases such as carbon dioxide from nitrogen [15,17], methane [16,17], and hydrogen [17] or hydrogen from nitrogen [17].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work gel membranes were developed based on Ion-Jelly Ò , a new gel material developed by Vidinha et al., which is obtained by adding an ionic liquid to gelatine [18]. This material is a transparent and flexible polymer which can be molded into a wide range of shapes, with the additional advantage of being obtained from a natural, biodegradable and cheap polymer (gelatine) and a compound which has no appreciable vapor pressure (ionic liquid). It was found that Ion-Jelly Ò can have very reasonable conductivities over a wide range of frequencies, leading to possible application in electrochemical devices. Recently, microfibers of Ion-Jelly Ò have been produced by electrospinning to obtain high surface area conductive materials [19].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we studied the application of the Ion-Jelly Ò membrane on the separation/fractionation of two systems in scCO2: the former model mixture of oleic acid and squalene, and the effluent stream resulting from the transesterification conversion of edible sunflower oil to methyl esters (i.e., biodiesel). To extend the possible range of applications of this kind of gel membranes, the application of the Ion-Jelly Ò membrane was also studied in the separation of low pressure gaseous systems.

The anions and cation structures of these ionic liquids are presented in Fig. 1. Gelatin was purchased from Panreac (Spain, ref. 403 902). The gases used in the experiments were hydrogen (high-purity grade (99.999%), Air Liquide, France), oxygen (high-purity grade (99.999%), Praxair, USA), nitrogen (industrial grade (99.99%), Praxair, USA), methane (99.95%, Praxair, USA) and carbon dioxide (high-purity grade (99.998%), Praxair, USA). The membranes were left to jellify overnight pressed between the glass plates, at room temperature. The ionic liquids chosen for this work were all based on the dicyanamide anion due to a limitation of the anions which are possible to choose to form Ion-Jelly Ò . With anions containing fluor atoms, jellification does not occur, which leaves out most of the more common anions. texts.orig: 2.3. scCO2 Fractionation experiments
texts.text: 2.3. scCO2 Fractionation experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The flow scheme of the high pressure apparatus for the fractionations experiments with scCO2 is presented in Fig. 2. Gaseous CO2 flows from a storage vessel into a gas compressor (model 5542121, Nova Swiss, Switzerland), where the working pressure is attained, and then into a Kenics static mixer (Chemineer, USA, model 37-04065) with an internal diameter of 4.928 mm, length of 178 mm, and 21 helical mixing elements. The liquid feed is introduced in the apparatus by means of a liquid pump (Minipump, LDC Analytical, FL, USA). The carbon dioxide and the oil mixture are put in contact in a ''tee'' joint just prior to the static mixer entrance. The gas and liquid phase flows pass co-currently through the static mixer where mixing of the two phases occur. At the liquid to gas flow ratios used in this work the static mixer is equivalent to one equilibrium stage of separation due to the co-current operation. Thus, in this way it is ensured that the gas phase leaving the static mixer ensemble and flowing to the membrane cell is saturated in oil at pressure and temperature operating conditions. The remaining liquid that is not solubilized in the CO2 phase is later collected in a separation vessel.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane module consists of a small flat sheet membrane test cell of 40 mm diameter, manufactured in stainless steel 316. It has entrance and exit ports in both the retentate and the permeate sides. The retentate and the permeate sides are both connected via a bypass valve which is kept open before starting the experiments to prevent undesired pressure differences across the membrane, which could damage it. On the retentate side, the pressure is controlled by a back-pressure regulator valve (model 27-1700, Tescom Europe GmbH & Co., Germany) and the outlet stream is depressurized into a collection vessel where the retentate liquid precipitates. On the permeate side, the pressure is controlled by a micrometric valve (60-11HF4V, HiP, USA) and the outlet stream is depressurized into a collection vessel where the permeate liquid precipitates. Gaseous CO2 is then collected from both retentate and permeate collection vessels and further recirculated. The working temperature in the entire installation is maintained by heating cables (HSS - 450 ° C, Horst GmbH, Germany) and respective temperature controllers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the fractionation experiments with the model mixture of squalene and oleic acid (50% in mass of each) we used the apparatus as described above. For the biodiesel experiments, a slight modification of the former configuration was used, in which two HPLC pumps (models 305 and 306, Gilson, USA) were employed, instead of using the single LDC pump, to pump separately the sunflower oil and the methanol flows. Furthermore, the separation vessel before the membrane cell was replaced by a high pressure packed-bed enzymatic tubular 316SS reactor (115 cm length, 9 mm I.D.) filled with Lipozyme Ò RM IM, which converted sunflower triglycerides and methanol to methyl esters and glycerol. The reaction mixture (monophasic) exiting the packed-bed reactor was fed to the membrane cell for further fractionation. The enzymatic reactor is heated by means of a water bath recirculated through an external shirt using a heating circulator (MD, Julabo Labortechnik GmbH, Germany).
texts.text: For the fractionation experiments with the model mixture of squalene and oleic acid (50% in mass of each) we used the apparatus as described above. For the biodiesel experiments, a slight modification of the former configuration was used, in which two HPLC pumps (models 305 and 306, Gilson, USA) were employed, instead of using the single LDC pump, to pump separately the sunflower oil and the methanol flows. Furthermore, the separation vessel before the membrane cell was replaced by a high pressure packed-bed enzymatic tubular 316SS reactor (115 cm length, 9 mm I.D.) filled with Lipozyme Ò RM IM, which converted sunflower triglycerides and methanol to methyl esters and glycerol. The reaction mixture (monophasic) exiting the packed-bed reactor was fed to the membrane cell for further fractionation. The enzymatic reactor is heated by means of a water bath recirculated through an external shirt using a heating circulator (MD, Julabo Labortechnik GmbH, Germany).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prior to the GC analysis, oleic acid was methylated to the respective methyl ester form, by reaction with diazomethane, according to the method described elsewhere [28]. Methyl heptadecanoate (TCI Europe, Belgium, >97.0%) was used as the internal standard. The chromatograph was a Thermo Quest Trace GC 2000 with a flame ionization detector (FID); a TR-Biodiesel (F) column, 30 m length x 0.25 mm I.D. and 0.25 l m film thickness from Thermo-Scientific was used. Helium was used as carrier at a constant flow of 2 mL min � 1 . The oven temperature was set at 393 K for 0.5 min, then to 493 K at 30 K min � 1 and holding for 1 min, and then to 523 K at 10 K min � 1 with a final holding time of 5 min. The injector was a PTV programmed to heat from 363 K to 533 K at 10 K s � 1 , with a transfer time of 3 min and cleaning at 633 K with a split of 250 mL min � 1 for 20 min. Detector temperature was 553 K. Peak identification was carried out using known standards (FAME mix C8-C24, Supelco).

texts.orig: The pure gas permeability of the Ion-Jelly Ò membranes for H2, N2, O2, CH4 and CO2 was determined by using the experimental apparatus shown in Fig. 3. This rig is composed by a stainless steel cell with two identical compartments separated by the membrane. The effective membrane area was 4 cm 2 . Each individual gas permeability was evaluated by pressurizing both compartments (feed and permeate) with the pure gas, and after opening the permeate outlet, establishing a driving force of around 0.07 MPa between the feed and the permeate compartments. The pressure change in both compartments over time was followed using two pressure transducers (Druck, PDCR 910 models 99166 and 991675, England). All measurements were performed at a constant temperature of 303 K by using a thermostatic bath (Julabo, Model EH, Germany).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Table 1 it is possible to observe, from the Young modulus, the difference in stiffness between spreading Ion-Jelly Ò over a cellulose support, or employing only gelatine. The Young modulus is the tangent modulus of the initial, linear portion of stress-strain curves, and increases with increasing stiffness of the material. It is seen that the presence of ionic liquid reduces the stiffness of the membrane, when compared with the cellulose support alone. When gelatine alone is used, on the other hand, there is a considerable increase in stiffness. This effect is visually apparent, as can be seen in Fig. 5, where the pictures of a membrane prepared only with gelatine and another prepared with Ion-Jelly Ò are shown. While the membrane prepared with Ion-Jelly Ò has a smooth surface and is flexible, the one prepared only with gelatine is wrinkled and stiff.

texts.orig: The curve profiles determined by sessile drop contact angle measurement (Fig. 6) are typical of a non-negligible porosity, which soaks the drop almost immediately upon contact. However, it can be observed that the IonJelly Ò membrane based in [EMIM][DCA] is more hydrophilic than the others, as it presents lower contact angles for water.

texts.orig: We have compared the permeability of scCO2 at 18.0 MPa and 313 K through the [BMIM][DCA] Ion-Jelly Ò membrane against the scCO2 permeability through several commercial reverse osmosis membranes [27]. We can see in Fig. 8 that the Ion-Jelly Ò membrane has a permeability to scCO2 similar to the commercial reverse osmosis membranes, although much higher pressure drops across the membrane can be obtained (up to 10 MPa), indicating that these membranes could be used for high pressure separations with scCO2 as solvent. The application of scCO2 to the [BMIM][DCA] membrane, does not appear to have any significant effect in the membrane morphology, when comparing Fig. 7c and d with Fig. 7e and f, respectively.

texts.orig: The first observation that it is possible to make from Fig. 10 is that the enzymatic transesterification of sunflower oil to biodiesel was not complete, since mono- and diacylglycerols were identified at the product stream coming from the enzymatic reactor and fed to the membrane cell. The calculated conversion of triacylglycerols was 100% while the yield of methyl esters was only of 24%. Nevertheless, the objective of this experiment was not to optimize the enzymatic process, but to test the membrane performance in non-ideal systems under continuous operation. Therefore a lower fatty acid methyl ester yield was required in order to access the viability of the membrane separation. The Ion-Jelly Ò membrane permeated preferentially the methyl esters and monoacylglycerols, while glycerol and diacylglycerols were retained. 90% in mass of the methyl esters produced in the reactor permeated through the membrane, while ca. 70% in mass of the monoacylglycerols were retained. On the other hand, the selectivity of the membrane towards the methyl esters in detriment of glycerol, a ME/G, was 166 in our experiment while that of methyl esters in detriment of monoglycerides a ME/MG, was 18.5. This result indicates that one could use this Ion-Jelly Ò membrane to achieve an efficient separation of the methyl esters from glycerol and other residual components.

texts.orig: 3.2.1. Elemental analysis
texts.text: 3.2.1. Elemental analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Elemental analysis was performed to feed, retentate and permeate streams of scCO2 fractionation experiments, to detect the presence of nitrogen. In the high pressure systems studied in this work, the nitrogen element is present only in the gelatine or ionic liquids structure. So, if nitrogen is detected in the retentate and permeate streams, it is indicative that the membrane is being degraded during operation. As can be seen from Table 2, only trace amounts of nitrogen were found in the permeate and retentate streams, similar to the nitrogen content present in the feed stream. After the scCO2 fractionation experiments, the stainless steel porous plate which lies beneath the membrane was disassembled and washed with ethanol in an ultrasounds bath to remove the remaining permeate trapped inside the pores of the plate. After ethanol evaporation, a sample of remaining liquid was also analyzed for the presence of nitrogen. A small concentration of nitrogen (from 0.06 to 0.29 in mass percentage) was detected, although negligible.

texts.orig: From Fig. 11, where the gas permeabilities of the Ion-Jelly Ò membranes are plotted against the Lennard-Jones diameter of the gas molecules [33], it can be seen that the gas permeabilities are size dependent, with the exception of CO2, which is the biggest molecule of all studied and has also the highest permeabilities for all the membranes tested. This is due to the high solubility of CO2, and since permeability is P = Di  Si , the effect of solubility is dominant. Table 3 shows the permeabilities of the pure gases in the Ion-Jelly Ò membranes and the calculated ideal selectivities, a i / j . It is noticed that the ideal selectivities are not very high, with the highest value obtained for the CO2/N2 separation with the [BMIM][DCA] membrane ( a CO2/N2 = 10), followed by the CO2/N2 separation with the [EMIM][DCA] membrane ( a CO2/N2 = 6.67) (Table 3). From Table 3 it is also seen that an increase in the imidazolium side chain size in the cation results in a decrease in the gas permeabilities.
texts.text: From Fig. 11, where the gas permeabilities of the Ion-Jelly Ò membranes are plotted against the Lennard-Jones diameter of the gas molecules [33], it can be seen that the gas permeabilities are size dependent, with the exception of CO2, which is the biggest molecule of all studied and has also the highest permeabilities for all the membranes tested. This is due to the high solubility of CO2, and since permeability is P = Di  Si , the effect of solubility is dominant. Table 3 shows the permeabilities of the pure gases in the Ion-Jelly Ò membranes and the calculated ideal selectivities, a i / j . It is noticed that the ideal selectivities are not very high, with the highest value obtained for the CO2/N2 separation with the [BMIM][DCA] membrane ( a CO2/N2 = 10), followed by the CO2/N2 separation with the [EMIM][DCA] membrane ( a CO2/N2 = 6.67) (Table 3). From Table 3 it is also seen that an increase in the imidazolium side chain size in the cation results in a decrease in the gas permeabilities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Comparing these results with previously obtained results for SILMs, we can see the permeabilities are in the same order of magnitude, but with lower ideal selectivities [14]. For instance, when comparing [EMIM][DCA] Ion-Jelly Ò membrane with literature data for [EMIM][DCA] SILM, the permeability to CO2 is slightly lower for the Ion-Jelly Ò case (482 and 610 barrer, respectively), whereas the selectivity for the CO2/N2 separation is 10 times lower (6,67 and 61, respectively) [5]. For the [BMIM][DCA] case, the permeability to CO2 is 200 barrer for the SILM [34] and 120 barrer for the Ion-Jelly Ò membrane, but the selectivity for the CO2/N2 separation is about eight times lower (78 for the SILM and 10 for the Ion-Jelly Ò membrane). Furthermore, we can observe that these membranes have permeabilities and ideal selectivities similar to polymeric materials. For instance, the ideal selectivity for CO2/CH4 of the [BMIM][DCA] Ion-Jelly Ò membrane is similar to the one of natural rubber (4.6) [35]. Comparing with other gel membranes, the permeabilities obtained in this work are identical to those obtained with different gels, but the selectivities are considerably lower. It is to be noticed, however, that the ionic liquids used in this work have not been used previously with other gels. With Pebax Ò 1657 and the IL 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium trifluoromethanesulfonate, a permeability to CO2 identical to that of the Ion-Jelly Ò with [BMPyr][DCA] was obtained (76.2 barrer), but with a CO2/N2 20 times higher [36]. Likewise, a permeability to CO2 similar to the one obtained with the Ion-Jelly Ò and [EMIM][DCA] was obtained with a PVDF membrane with the IL 1-ethyl-3-methylimida- zolium tetracyanoborate (467 barrer), but with a CO2/N2 about five times higher [37].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Green and sustainable gel membranes based in a biodegradable biopolymer (gelatine) with incorporated RTILS (Ion-Jelly Ò membranes) were developed, characterized and tested for the fractionation of several mixtures.
texts.text: Green and sustainable gel membranes based in a biodegradable biopolymer (gelatine) with incorporated RTILS (Ion-Jelly Ò membranes) were developed, characterized and tested for the fractionation of several mixtures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane of [EMIM][DCA] Ion-Jelly Ò revealed to be more hydrophilic than the membranes prepared with [BMIM][DCA] and [BMPyr][DCA]. The permeability to scCO2 is similar to the ones of commercial reverse osmosis membranes, although higher pressure drops can be obtained. From SEM images it is possible to see that Ion-Jelly Ò penetrates the cellulose support, covering the cellulose fibers and occupying the empty spaces between them, and that the application of scCO2 presents no significative change in the membrane morphology.
texts.text: The membrane of [EMIM][DCA] Ion-Jelly Ò revealed to be more hydrophilic than the membranes prepared with [BMIM][DCA] and [BMPyr][DCA]. The permeability to scCO2 is similar to the ones of commercial reverse osmosis membranes, although higher pressure drops can be obtained. From SEM images it is possible to see that Ion-Jelly Ò penetrates the cellulose support, covering the cellulose fibers and occupying the empty spaces between them, and that the application of scCO2 presents no significative change in the membrane morphology.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ion-Jelly Ò membranes presented no appreciable fractionation for the model mixture of oleic acid and squalene. In the fractionation of products and reactants from an enzymatic transesterification reaction, they presented very good retention factors for glycerol and monoglycerides. Elemental analysis showed that these membranes are not degraded with operation in scCO2.
texts.text: Ion-Jelly Ò membranes presented no appreciable fractionation for the model mixture of oleic acid and squalene. In the fractionation of products and reactants from an enzymatic transesterification reaction, they presented very good retention factors for glycerol and monoglycerides. Elemental analysis showed that these membranes are not degraded with operation in scCO2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When testing the Ion-Jelly Ò membranes for the separation of gases, it was found that the permeabilities were similar to those reported in the literature for the corresponding SILMs. However, the selectivities were lower when compared with those obtained with SILMs.
